# Adamant Protocol — Full Index

This document inlines the most important content of the Adamant project's marketing site and key whitepaper sections, so AI tools can index the protocol's design in a single fetch. The canonical source is the whitepaper repository at https://github.com/adamant-protocol/adamant.

The protocol is in specification phase. No chain is running yet.

---

## 1. What Adamant is

Adamant is a Layer 1 blockchain protocol designed to serve users who require properties that no existing programmable chain delivers in combination: privacy by default, high transaction throughput, sub-second finality at design-target validator count, hybrid post-quantum cryptographic security, phone-verifiable correctness, and credibly neutral governance with no foundation, no premine, no admin keys, and no upgrade authority after genesis.

The protocol combines a directed-acyclic-graph (DAG) consensus mechanism, an object-based parallel execution model, encrypted transaction propagation (threshold-encrypted at design-target validator count, time-lock-encrypted via a publicly verifiable VDF during the low-N period), recursive zero-knowledge proofs of state validity, and a fixed constitutional rule set established at genesis. Every transaction is shielded by default, with users retaining the ability to selectively disclose specific information through cryptographically verifiable view keys.

The chain rejects on-chain governance by design. The protocol's consensus rules, virtual machine, token issuance schedule, and validity logic are fixed at genesis and cannot be modified by any party, including the original implementers. Changes require socially-coordinated forks in which every node operator individually opts into new client software.

Adamant commits to a sustained throughput floor of at least 50,000 transactions per second on a single shard at design-target validator count, a finality latency of approximately 500 milliseconds for transactions not requiring shared-state consensus, and a per-transaction fee floor on the order of $0.0001 USD-equivalent at design throughput. The 50,000 TPS figure is a floor, not a cap — actual throughput depends on the active set's aggregate hardware capability and current network conditions, and routinely exceeds the floor when validators run better-than-baseline hardware. The floor is calibrated to keep validators on residential-fibre commodity-desktop hardware (rather than VPS-grade infrastructure) and is subject to empirical validation before genesis. The reduction from earlier 200,000 TPS targets is the engineering cost of preserving low-coordination launch and broad participation.

The active validator set is dynamic, with a constitutional floor of 7 validators (the smallest size at which Byzantine fault tolerance retains margin against correlated failures) and a soft ceiling of 75 (the upper bound at which DAG-BFT communication cost remains tractable on residential-fibre hardware at the throughput floor). Selection follows a first-come-first-served-with-persistent-membership rule: validators are admitted in registration order, and once admitted retain their slot continuously until they fail liveness duties or voluntarily unbond. This rewards commitment and continuity rather than hardware budget or stake size — early committed home-fibre validators cannot be displaced by wealthier latecomers or by faster competitors. The chain self-activates the moment 7 validators are simultaneously registered, stake-eligible, and online; there is no coordination event, no recruited genesis cohort, no foundation. Below the floor, the chain halts rather than forks, preserving safety at the cost of liveness during periods of severe validator unavailability. The chain commits an on-chain security-tier signal (Tier I at 7–14 validators, Tier II at 15–29, Tier III at 30+) so that wallets and applications can adapt to the chain's current security posture.

Participation is structured across four tiers with bounded power. Validators do consensus and mempool decryption. Provers do steady-state recursive-proof generation in a permissionless market, paid per-proof from transaction fees; validators do proof generation as a fallback at degraded cadence to preserve phone-verifiability regardless of prover-market health. Witnesses perform attestation, data availability sampling, recursive proof verification, and fraud detection on phone-class hardware. Service nodes provide light-client infrastructure. The role split keeps validator hardware on residential-fiber commodity desktops by moving GPU-class proof generation off-tier; phone-class participation is meaningful via witnesses; no single tier alone controls the chain.

The protocol uses standard, peer-reviewed cryptographic primitives — Ed25519 and ML-DSA (CRYSTALS-Dilithium, FIPS 204) for signatures in a hybrid configuration (Ed25519 for ordinary transactions and validator messages; ML-DSA for identity-binding operations and high-value opt-in), ML-KEM-768 (FIPS 203) for post-quantum key agreement underlying stealth addresses and encrypted memos, BLS12-381 for signature aggregation, SHA-3 for hashing, the Halo 2 proving system for zero-knowledge proofs, and a Wesolowski Verifiable Delay Function over class groups for time-lock encryption during the low-N period. No novel cryptography is introduced. The contribution is the systems-level synthesis of these primitives into a coherent architecture.

---

## 2. Terminology

The protocol uses one friendly alias on the marketing site; the other tier names are used in their spec form:

- **Node Runners** = validators (whitepaper). Operators of the active set running consensus + mempool decryption on residential-fiber hardware.
- **Witnesses** = whitepaper §8.7.2. Phone-class participants performing attestation, data availability sampling, proof verification, and fraud / reordering detection.
- **Provers** = whitepaper §8.5.3. GPU-class operators producing recursive proofs in a permissionless market.
- **Service Nodes** = whitepaper §9.10. Permissionless light-client infrastructure providers.
- **ADM** = the native token. Native to the protocol layer (not contract-defined), divisible to nine decimal places, used for fees and staking.

---

## 3. The seven properties Adamant combines

Each property below has been demonstrated in production by some chain. No single chain currently combines more than three.

| Property | Best demonstrated by | Year |
|---|---|---|
| 200k+ TPS, sub-second finality | Mysticeti / Sui | 2024 |
| Programmable privacy | Aztec | 2025 |
| Encrypted mempool (in production) | Shutter / Gnosis | 2024 |
| Phone-verifiable chain | Mina | 2021 |
| Native account abstraction | zkSync Era, StarkNet, Aptos | 2022–24 |
| Post-quantum signatures | NIST standardisation | 2024 |
| Credible neutrality | Bitcoin, Monero | 2009, 2014 |

Adamant is designed to combine all seven, with credible neutrality enforced at genesis. Adamant's throughput floor is 50,000+ TPS at design-target validator count rather than the 200,000+ demonstrated by Mysticeti / Sui — calibrated to keep validators on residential-fibre commodity-desktop hardware rather than VPS-grade infrastructure. The 50,000 figure is a floor, not a cap; actual throughput routinely exceeds it on better-than-baseline hardware.

---

## 4. The eight design principles (whitepaper §2)

The principles are listed in priority order. Where two conflict, the higher-numbered yields to the lower.

**Principle I — Credible neutrality.** The protocol MUST NOT admit any party with the unilateral capability to alter its operation. No on-chain governance. No foundation, treasury, or admin keys. No premine or founder allocation. Forks require individual opt-in. No protected contracts or precompiles favouring the implementers.

**Principle II — Privacy by default.** Transactions on the protocol MUST be private by default. Users MUST retain the ability to selectively disclose specific information to specific parties through cryptographically verifiable means. No backdoor decryption. MEV protection is structural at design-target validator count and bounded-but-non-zero in the time-lock regime during the low-N period (honestly disclosed via the security tier mechanism).

**Principle III — Verifiability without trust.** Any participant MUST be able to verify the correctness of the protocol's operation using only consumer-grade hardware, without trusting any third party. Phone-verifiable verification via recursive zero-knowledge proofs. No light-client trust assumptions. The protocol commits to a steady-state proof cadence (driven by a permissionless prover market) and a fallback cadence (driven by validators on their own hardware at degraded but bounded freshness windows); phone-verifiability never depends on a market materialising.

**Principle IV — Performance sufficient for use.** The protocol MUST provide throughput, latency, and cost properties consistent with practical use. 50,000+ TPS floor (not a cap) on a single shard at design-target validator count on residential-fibre hardware — actual throughput routinely exceeds the floor on better hardware. Subject to empirical validation pre-genesis. ~500ms finality at design-N. $0.0001 USD-equivalent fee floor. 10–30s inclusion latency during low-N period (time-lock regime), an acknowledged consequence of operating without coordinated DKG.

**Principle V — Mutability is a property of objects, not the chain.** The chain's consensus rules MUST be immutable. Objects MAY declare their own mutability rules at creation, and those declarations MUST themselves be immutable.

**Principle VI — Standard primitives, novel synthesis.** The protocol MUST use standard, peer-reviewed cryptographic primitives. The protocol MUST NOT introduce novel cryptographic constructions. Innovation is at the systems layer, not the primitive layer.

**Principle VII — Permissionless participation.** Participation MUST NOT require permission from any party.

**Principle VIII — Post-quantum security at identity and privacy layers.** The protocol MUST be post-quantum secure at the identity layer (addresses, validator registrations, contract deployments) and at the privacy layer's key-agreement surface (stealth addresses, encrypted memos). Ordinary transaction signatures MAY use Ed25519 for performance reasons; users MUST retain the ability to opt into ML-DSA per-transaction. Trade-off honestly disclosed: ordinary transaction signatures are quantum-forgeable; identity, structural state, and historical privacy are not.

---

## 5. The five mechanisms (whitepaper §3, §8, §9, §11)

### Mechanism 01: DAG consensus (§8.3)

Mysticeti-based DAG. The active set is dynamic — a constitutional floor of 7 Node Runners (validators) and a soft ceiling of 75 calibrated to residential-fibre hardware. Selection is first-come-first-served with persistent membership: admitted in registration order, slots held continuously until liveness failure or voluntary unbonding. This rewards commitment over stake size or hardware budget. Each Node Runner publishes a vertex every 250 ms, each referencing 2N/3+1 of the prior round. Order emerges deterministically from the structure itself; commit waves finalise blocks every 4 rounds. Throughput floor ≥50,000 TPS at design-target N (not a cap — actual throughput routinely exceeds it on better-than-baseline hardware), finality ~500 ms. The chain self-activates the moment 7 are simultaneously online; at the floor, the chain halts on disagreement rather than forks.

Notation: V_i^{r+1} → references ⌈2N/3⌉ + 1 vertices in round r.

### Mechanism 02: Two-regime encrypted mempool (§3.6, §3.8, §8.4)

At N≥15, users encrypt transactions to the upcoming epoch's BLS threshold key; Node Runners include and order encrypted blobs during epoch E; at the E→E+1 boundary, 2N/3+1 publish decryption shares as a side-effect of vertex production; decrypted transactions execute in epoch E+1. MEV protection is structural — front-running and sandwiching are cryptographically impossible.

At N<15, users encrypt to a Wesolowski VDF puzzle (operative because threshold encryption requires coordinated DKG that doesn't function at very low N). The rotated round anchor (selected per-round by the consensus VRF) decrypts atomically with their vertex. Mitigations: deterministic anchor rotation prevents self-selection; decryption-publication binding prevents cross-vertex front-running; equivocation is slashable at 100% of stake. A bounded intra-anchor reordering surface remains and is honestly disclosed rather than hidden.

Hysteresis prevents flapping: switch to threshold at N≥15, switch back to time-lock at N<10.

### Mechanism 03: Recursive zero-knowledge proofs (§8.5)

A Halo 2 recursive proof at every epoch boundary attests to the validity of all transactions and state transitions from genesis to the present. Each new proof π_n verifies the prior proof π_{n−1} plus the current epoch's transactions. The total proof is one constant-size artifact (≈5–10 KB) verifiable in 50–200 ms on a smartphone — by anyone, with no light-client trust assumption.

Proofs are produced by a permissionless prover market at steady state (GPU-class operators race to submit valid proofs and win the per-proof bounty; no stake, no application, no approval). If no prover submits within ~2–5 s, validators take over fallback proof generation at degraded cadence (~5 s vs sub-second). Phone-verifiability never depends on the prover market materialising.

Notation: π_n = Prove(C_chain ; π_{n−1}, txs_E); Verify(π_n) → {0, 1}.

### Mechanism 04: Constitutional immutability (§11.1)

No on-chain governance. No admin keys. No upgrade authority. The fixing of the protocol is enforced not by a foundation, not by a multisig, not by an on-chain governance mechanism, but by the structural absence of any mechanism to modify the protocol. Change requires a published spec revision, a public reference implementation, and 2/3+1-by-stake of Node Runners each independently choosing to run new software. Validators that don't upgrade simply stay on the existing chain.

### Mechanism 05: Service-node infrastructure market (§9.10, §10.5.5)

A standardised query protocol opens a permissionless market for service nodes that serve Witnesses and other lightweight clients. No registry, no allocation; anyone can run one. Node Runners may, at their discretion, fund infrastructure providers from their commission revenue to compete for delegators on service quality. Issuance flows only to Node Runners and Witnesses; the protocol enables the market without funding it from issuance. Provers are fee-funded; service nodes are fee/ecosystem-funded.

Notation: q ∈ {get_state, get_proof, submit_tx}; receipt = σ_sn(q, response).

---

## 6. Genesis and economics (whitepaper §10, §11)

### The genesis pool

A fixed 100,000,000 ADM exists at genesis as a protocol-level construct. It is not held by any party — no foundation, no multisig, no privileged address. The pool is a counter that decrements only through public acquisition paths:

- **Path A — Burn-to-mint (70M ADM):** Anyone burns BTC or ETH at a verifiable null address on the source chain. The protocol observes the confirmed burn and mints the corresponding ADM at the protocol-defined rate.
- **Path B — Node Runner block rewards (30M ADM):** Earned block-by-block by Node Runners running the chain.

A per-address claim cap on Path A (1% → 8% over months 0–12, then uncapped) prevents early concentration. The launch phase ends when the pool exhausts or at five years, whichever comes first. Unclaimed tokens at the time cap are destroyed.

The chain self-activates the moment 7 Node Runners are simultaneously registered, stake-eligible, and online. There is no recruited genesis cohort. There is no human-in-the-loop activation. Path A produces the initial stake distribution; once 7 validators are online, block production and Path B begin.

### Post-launch issuance (§10.3)

After the launch phase terminates, the protocol issues new ADM continuously to validators as block rewards. The schedule is fixed at genesis:

- Years 1–5: 4% of total supply per year
- Years 6–10: 3%
- Years 11–20: 2%
- Year 21+: 1%, in perpetuity

Distribution: the substantial majority is distributed across Node Runners proportional to bonded stake (including delegations), then split between validator and delegators per commission rate (typically 5–15%). A small slice (~5%) flows to the Witness compensation pool, distributed across phone-class participants. Provers and service nodes are fee-funded; they receive no issuance. No portion goes to a foundation, treasury, or development fund.

### Fee mechanism (§10.4)

Multi-dimensional fees: computation, state storage, state rent, bandwidth, proof verification, and proof generation are priced independently. Each dimension uses EIP-1559-style price discovery with per-epoch adjustment. Base fees are burned; tips go to Node Runners; the proof-generation fee dimension funds the prover bounty pool; a slice of the verification fee dimension funds witness compensation.

Under typical usage, fee burn approximately equals issuance — supply is roughly stable. Heavy usage produces net deflation. Light usage produces modest inflation.

### What does not happen at genesis (§10.2.1)

No founder allocation, no foundation treasury, no pre-mine for development funding, no early-investor allocation, no validator set "starter pack," no ecosystem fund, no marketing fund, no custodian holding the genesis pool, no multisig or governance mechanism controlling the pool's release.

---

## 7. Constitutional commitments fixed at genesis (whitepaper §11)

At genesis, the protocol's specification — every section of the whitepaper, the parameters within it, and the reference implementation — is fixed. There is no party with the authority to:

- Add a new validity rule
- Change a gas cost
- Modify the issuance schedule
- Add a new system contract
- Freeze any account or transaction
- Censor any participant
- Pause the chain
- Issue an emergency fix
- Coordinate an automatic upgrade
- Change consensus parameters (active-set floor / ceiling, round duration, epoch length, slashing rates)

Each of these operations is technically possible — anyone can write a modified Rust implementation that does any of them. What is impossible is for any party to make such modifications take effect on the running network without the explicit cooperation of the Node Runners running the existing implementation.

The protocol additionally enables, but does not fund from a dedicated issuance allocation, a permissionless service-node infrastructure market and a validator-funded infrastructure mechanism. Constitutional commitments include: the eight principles, the cryptographic primitives (including Ed25519 / ML-DSA hybrid posture and ML-KEM-768 for privacy key agreement), the dynamic active-set bounds (floor 7, soft ceiling 75) with first-come-first-served selection and persistent membership, the two-regime mempool with hysteresis, the four-tier participation model, the validator-fallback proof generation, the security-tier disclosure, the genesis pool mechanism, the post-launch issuance schedule, and the structure of issuance flow (validators and witnesses receive slices; provers and service nodes are fee/ecosystem-funded).

---

## 8. Common questions

**Q: What is Adamant?**
A: A Layer 1 blockchain protocol designed for privacy by default, sub-second finality at design-target validator count, phone-verifiable correctness, and credibly neutral governance — with no foundation, no admin keys, no upgrade authority, and no premine.

**Q: Why 50,000 TPS rather than 200,000?**
A: A 200,000 TPS target on a DAG-BFT mechanism implies an active set of approximately 200 validators, which in turn implies VPS-grade hardware at every validator. That hardware floor excludes the residential-fibre operators the protocol is designed to include. A 50,000 TPS floor tolerates an active-set ceiling of 75 on consumer-desktop hardware on residential fibre. The reduction in headline throughput is the engineering cost of preserving low-coordination launch and broad participation; 50,000 TPS remains substantially above the sustained throughput of any production L1 in 2026 and is an order of magnitude above any consumer payment workload. Note: 50,000 is a floor, not a cap. Actual throughput depends on the active set's aggregate hardware and network conditions, and routinely exceeds the floor when validators run better-than-baseline hardware.

**Q: Is there a token I can buy?**
A: No. The protocol is in specification phase. No chain is running. No token has been issued. There has been no token sale. There never will be a private allocation, ICO, or VC round.

**Q: When does Adamant launch?**
A: There is no launch date. The whitepaper is a draft, public for review. The reference implementation has not yet begun. A realistic timeline for one or two engineers is 24–36 months; for a small team, 18–24 months. The protocol's value is in being right at genesis, not in being early.

**Q: Who runs Adamant?**
A: No company, no foundation, no team. The protocol cannot pivot, run out of funding, or be acquired because no entity owns it. The original implementers will receive zero ADM at genesis. Distribution begins only through the public acquisition paths (burn-to-mint and Node Runner block rewards). The chain was designed and built by Ryan Geldart; no party retains protocol-level powers post-genesis.

**Q: How does the chain launch with no foundation and no recruited genesis cohort?**
A: The chain self-activates the moment 7 validators are simultaneously registered, stake-eligible, and online. There is no coordination event. No human-in-the-loop activation. Path A of the genesis pool (burn-to-mint) bootstraps the initial validators: the first burners receive ADM, register as validators, and once 7 are online the chain produces its first block. Below 7, the chain is dormant. From 7 onward, block production proceeds normally.

**Q: What happens at low validator count (N=7–14)?**
A: The chain operates with weaker guarantees and discloses them honestly. The mempool uses time-lock encryption (Wesolowski VDF) instead of threshold encryption (which requires coordinated DKG that doesn't function at very low N); MEV protection has a bounded intra-anchor reordering surface that doesn't exist at design-target N. The chain halts on disagreement rather than forks — safety preserved at the cost of liveness during severe-unavailability periods. An on-chain security tier signal (Tier I) tells wallets and applications to apply higher confirmation thresholds. As N grows past 15, the chain transitions to Tier II (threshold encryption); past 30, Tier III (full design-target).

**Q: Is the chain post-quantum?**
A: At the surfaces that matter most. ML-DSA (FIPS 204) protects identity-binding operations: account addresses, validator registrations, contract deployments, opt-in high-value transactions. ML-KEM-768 (FIPS 203) protects key agreement for stealth addresses and encrypted memos — historical privacy survives the quantum threshold. Ordinary user-transaction signatures use Ed25519 by default for bandwidth reasons (Ed25519 is 64 bytes; ML-DSA-65 is ~3.3 KB; the difference is structural for residential-fiber validator participation). The trade-off is honestly disclosed: ordinary transaction signatures are quantum-forgeable, affecting transaction-history forensics but not chain integrity or privacy. Wallets default to ML-DSA above a user-configurable value threshold.

**Q: How does it differ from Ethereum?**
A: Privacy by default rather than transparent by default. DAG consensus rather than single-leader. Recursive ZK verification on a phone rather than full nodes or hosted RPC. Hybrid post-quantum security rather than classical-only. No foundation rather than the Ethereum Foundation. No on-chain governance rather than EIP coordination.

**Q: How does it differ from Sui?**
A: Adamant is Mysticeti-based but adds a two-regime threshold/time-lock encrypted mempool, recursive proofs for phone-verifiability, hybrid post-quantum signatures from genesis, a four-tier participation model (validators, provers, witnesses, service nodes), and credibly neutral governance. Sui is foundation-controlled; Adamant has no foundation.

**Q: How does it differ from Aztec?**
A: Aztec is an Ethereum L2 with programmable privacy. Adamant is a sovereign L1 with the same privacy primitive plus high throughput, phone-verifiable correctness, and credible neutrality. Aztec inherits Ethereum's settlement guarantees and rollup framework constraints; Adamant is independently sovereign.

**Q: How does it differ from Bitcoin?**
A: Bitcoin's design philosophy — credibly neutral, capture-resistant, settlement-grade — extended with programmable smart contracts, default privacy, and sub-second finality. The 2026 reading of the original thesis.

**Q: Is the code open source?**
A: Yes. Apache 2.0. The repository is at https://github.com/adamant-protocol/adamant. The implementation is forthcoming; the whitepaper is the canonical source for now.

**Q: Can users running smartphones really verify the chain?**
A: Yes. A Halo 2 recursive zero-knowledge proof at every epoch boundary compresses chain history into a constant-size artifact (~5–10 KB) verifiable in 50–200 ms on a consumer phone. Proofs are produced by a permissionless prover market at steady state; if the market is insufficient, validators take over fallback proof generation at degraded cadence. Phone-verifiability never depends on the market materialising.

**Q: What stops a foundation from forming after genesis?**
A: Nothing stops people from forming a foundation. What's prevented is the foundation having any technical capability to alter the protocol. No admin keys exist. No on-chain governance exists. No upgrade authority exists. The protocol's specification is what runs; any change requires socially-coordinated forks where each Node Runner individually opts in.

**Q: Who is this for?**
A: Users whose threat model includes their own bank, their own protocol foundation, or their own jurisdiction — and applications whose correctness must outlive the institutions that hosted them at deployment. If existing chains adequately serve a user's needs, Adamant is not for them.

---
